title: Lesson 338
transcript: The next piece we have to talk about here related to power into a building is different types of power degradation or power outages. And we've got this nice little table here because there's kind of six major power degradation or power outages that you need to know about. And they all kind of relate to whether it's over a very short period of time or over a very long period of time, and whether it's no power, not enough power, or too much power. So hopefully this is a good way of helping you memorize this and summarize these different types of power outages or degradations. So let's start with no power. No power for a very short period of time. And by the way, when we say short period of time, we're meaning usually just a few milliseconds, a handful of milliseconds. No power for a very short period of time is called a fault. And no power for a long period of time is called a blackout. So a blackout would be where you, you know, seconds, minutes, hours, days of no power. That's a blackout. Next, let's move down to not enough power. And by not enough power, what we usually mean there is that the voltage is too low. So like here in North America, if you plug something into the wall, you should see somewhere between 110 to 120 volts. In a low power situation, you might see 80 volts coming out of the wall. That would be not enough power. Not enough power for a very short period of time can be known as a sag or a dip. So say low voltage for just a few milliseconds, you would call that a sag or a dip. and then not enough power for a long period of time is known as a brownout. An interesting little bit of trivia here, brownouts are usually intentional. So if a power company realizes like, uh-oh, it's like a really hot summer day, everyone's running their air conditioners and we're not able to produce enough power, and we're about to have a blackout, what the power company might do is actually lower the voltage. And yeah, that is going to cause all sorts of mayhem, and it's going to cause all sorts of electrical devices to fail, especially computer equipment. But some stuff will keep running. And so the idea is, you know, a brownout is still slightly better than a complete blackout. So that's a brownout. Not enough power for a long period of time. Seconds, minutes, days. And then the final power degradation issue we have to talk about here is too much power. So imagine a lightning strike or something like that. And ultimately what too much power is, is it's usually too much voltage. So instead of getting, you know, like 110 volts out of the wall, maybe you get 500 volts or a thousand volts or something like that. Now too much power over a very short period of time for just a few milliseconds is known as a spike. So a spike is too much power for a very short period of time. And then a surge is too much power for a long period of time. So if you're expecting 110 out of your wall socket and you're getting 200 volts for the entire day, that would be a surge. And obviously is going to very much damage equipment as well. So a surge is too much power over a long period of time. Thank you.
